segments.s095-g9: Word order is a bit compressed in classical Persian: سر مرد جنگی = "the head of the fighting man/warrior" (سر + [مردِ جنگی], ezāfe omitted). خرد is the object/idea ('wisdom'), and نسپرد is the past negative of سپردن (ن + سپرد), meaning 'did not entrust/give over' — here idiomatically 'did not submit/yield'.
segments.s095-g10: که introduces the explanatory clause ('for / because'). هرگز is the adverb 'never'. نیامیخت is the negative past of آمیختن (ن + آمیخت) 'did not mingle/associate'. کین 'vengeance/hatred' is the thing that 'never mingled' with خرد 'wisdom'; با is the preposition 'with'.
segments.s095-g11: چنین 'thus' modifies the speech act. داد is the past verb 'gave' (he gave). پاسخ is the direct object 'an answer'; the preposition به marks the addressee 'to Afrasiab'. Classical word order allows verb before its object.
segments.s095-g12: که introduces the reported content ('that'). لختی = 'for a short while'. بباید is the modal 'one must / it is necessary' (present/future modal). همی is an emphatic/continuative particle here ('indeed/just' or slightly emphatic). شرم و آب are the nouns 'shame and water' — poetically the clause omits an explicit verb after بباید, so the sense is 'one must suffer/endure (شرم و آب) for a short while' (i.e. endure shame and thirst); this ellipsis is common in verse.
segments.s095-g13: هر آنگه is an adverbial 'whenever / if ever'. کت 'coat' is the subject; آید is the (present/generic) verb 'comes' or 'falls'. به + دسترس 'into reach' is the prepositional complement; بد qualifies دسترس as 'bad / evil' — 'if a coat comes into evil hands/into bad reach'.
segments.s095-g14: ز یزدان بترس is an imperative: ز (poetic 'from'/'because of') + یزدان 'God', and بترس 'fear!' (imperative). و مکن بد به کس is a negative imperative construction: مکن 'do not do', بد 'bad / harm', به کس 'to anyone' — 'do not harm anyone'.
segments.s095-g15: که here introduces the explanatory/subordinate clause ('for/that'). تاج و کمر = 'the crown and the belt' (symbols of rank and strength) is the compound subject. چون تو بیند 'when/if they see you' (چون = when/if/like; تو = you; بیند = 'see' — classical usage may use singular verb form). بسی = 'enough / sufficient' — the clause means 'if crown and belt see you, that alone suffices.'
segments.s095-g16: نخواهد شدن is a future/irrealis construction meaning 'will not become' (نخواهد used as future/modal + شدن 'to become'). رام 'tame' is the predicate complement: 'will not become tame'. با هر کسی 'by/with any person' — 'cannot be tamed by any person.'
segments.s095-g33: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is a past (3rd‑person) verb 'he heard' and here begins a subordinate temporal clause; the subject افراسیاب follows the verb (typical flexible word order in classical Persian). این سخن is the direct object 'this/these words'.
segments.s095-g34: که introduces the content clause (‘that/what’ he heard). دستان جنگی is a genitive/nominal phrase 'the hands (forces) of war' with جنگی as an attributive adjective. چه here is exclamatory/interrogative 'how/what (a...)'. افگند is a past (3rd‑person) verb 'threw/cast' and بن is its object 'foundation(s)/base' — together conveying 'how the warlike hands had cast down (the) foundations'.
segments.s095-g35: بیاورد is a past (3rd‑person) verb 'he brought' or 'he led'. لشکر is the direct object 'an army/host'. سوی is the preposition 'toward'. خوار ری here names the destination (a place, ‘Khwar/Khvar’ and Rey); خوار functions as part of the place name rather than its lexical sense.
segments.s095-g36: بیاراست is a past verb 'he arrayed/put in order' (i.e., prepared). جنگ is the object 'war/battle' or shorthand for 'for battle'. و is the coordinating 'and'. بیفشارد is a past verb 'he pressed/advanced (upon)' and پی is an adverbial particle 'after/in pursuit', so the clause means 'he prepared for battle and pressed on in pursuit'.
segments.s095-g37: طلایه is the subject 'vanguard/forefront'. شب و روز is an idiomatic temporal phrase 'night and day' (i.e., continually). در جنگ بود places the subject 'in battle' with بود as the past copula 'was'.
segments.s095-g38: تو گفتی is 'you said' (past 2nd‑person); که introduces reported content. گیتی بر او تنگ بود is a predicative clause 'the world was narrow for him' with بر ... تنگ بودن meaning 'to be too narrow/tight for (someone)'; بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s095-g39: مبارز is 'fighter/combatant' (subject). بسی here means 'many' or 'a great number' (classical use) rather than its listed senses, so marked accordingly. کشته شد is a passive construction 'were killed' (کشته past participle + شد as auxiliary). بر دو روی literally 'on the two sides' = 'on both sides', with بر as the preposition and روی as 'side/surface'.
segments.s095-g40: همه is the quantifier 'all/everyone'. نامداران is a plural adjective/noun 'the renowned/illustrious ones'. پرخاشجوی is an adjective 'quarrelsome/aggressive', modifying نامداران; together 'all the renowned were quarrelsome'.
segments.s095-g25: چنین گفت = “he said thus/so,” with the adverb چنین (= thus) before the verb گفت (past tense ‘said’). که‌اکنون introduces the reported clause (‘that now’); که here functions as the subordinating conjunction and اکنون is the temporal adverb “now.” سر بخت اوی is a compact genitive phrase (literally “head/top of his fortune”): سر + بخت = “apex/height of fortune,” and اوی is the archaic/poetic possessive meaning “his/her.”
segments.s095-g26: شود is the present/subjunctive/future form of شدن ‘to become’ (here translated as “will become” or “will be”), starting the predicate of the reported clause. تار و ویران‌شود is a coordinated pair of predicates (tār … and be-ruined), with و joining them; ویران‌شود is another verbal form of شدن with a passive/medium sense “be ruined.” تخت اوی is the noun phrase “his throne” placed after the verbs for poetic word-order; اوی again is the possessive “his.”
segments.s095-g27: بزد is the simple past “he struck/struck at.” نای رویین is the object phrase ‘the (metal) reed/trumpet’ where رویین (literally ‘iron‑like’/‘metal’) qualifies نای. The conjunction و links the second verb phrase بربست کوس; in this poetic context بربست functions as a past verb coordinating with بزد (rendered here as ‘beat/sounded’), and کوس is the drum (war-drum).
segments.s095-g28: بیاراست here is a transitive verb ‘he arrayed/adorned’ and governs لشکر ‘the army’ as its object. چو is the comparative particle ‘like/as,’ followed by the simile چشم خروس ‘the (eye) of a rooster.’ The whole phrase means he set the army in order, bright or conspicuous like a rooster’s eye; word order (verb before object) and the simile marker are typical of Ferdowsi’s poetic style.
segments.s095-g29: سپهبد is the subject (title ‘spahbed’). سوی پارس is a prepositional/directional phrase ‘toward Pars,’ and بنهاد (past of بنهادن) is the verb ‘he set/placed’ used idiomatically with روی ‘face’ to mean ‘he turned/set his face toward.’ روی is the object (face) placed after the verb for emphasis — a common poetic order.
segments.s095-g30: همی‌رفت is the imperfective past ‘was going / proceeded’ (the prefix همی indicates continuous or habitual action in classical Persian). پرخشم is a predicate adjective ‘full of wrath / furious,’ and the coordinated phrase و دل کینه‌جوی appends ‘and (with) a heart that seeks revenge,’ where کینه‌جوی is an adjectival/participial compound meaning ‘vengeful’ or ‘revenge‑seeking.’
segments.s095-g31: ز دریا به دریا is a poetic locative phrase ‘from sea to sea’ (ز = from, به = to/toward). همی before the predicate adds continuous/emphatic aspect, and مرد بود is ‘he was a man’ with بود as the past auxiliary ‘was.’ The compact order (locative phrase before subject-predicate) is typical of epic diction.
segments.s095-g32: رخ ماه و خورشید is a comparative/metaphorical noun phrase ‘a face (like) the moon and the sun’ (i.e., beautiful/bright). پر here is the adjective/former ‘full (of),’ and گرد بود is a predicative construction with بود ‘was’ — in context the compound describes his face as full/rounded like moon and sun (poetic compression of adjectival meanings).
segments.s095-g17: This is a two-part nominal clause: two parallel descriptive phrases introduced by یکی (‘one’). پر with the genitive marker ز forms an adjectival phrase “پر ز آتش” = “full of fire.” The second phrase is a compound adjective پرخرد (“full of wisdom/wise”). The word-order is noun/adjective phrases side-by-side for contrast.
segments.s095-g18: خرد is the abstract noun 'wisdom' as the topic; با marks the adversary/instrumental relation 'with' and سرِ دیو is the object of that relation ('the demon's head'). کی is an interrogative 'when/how' introducing a rhetorical question, and درخورد here is a classical/past-form sense meaning 'befit / come to grips with / be suitable (to meet)'; the clause asks how wisdom could possibly match the demon's head.
segments.s095-g19: سپهبد is the subject (the commander). برآشفت is a past tense poetic verb 'grew angry/was enraged.' چون پیل مست is a simile ('like a drunken elephant'), with چون introducing the likeness and the adjective مست qualifying پیل.
segments.s095-g20: به پاسخ is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'in answer / in reply.' The following prepositional phrase به شمشیر ('toward/at the sword' or 'to his sword') indicates direction, and یازید دست is a poetic word-order where the past verb یازید ('drew forth/broke forth') governs the object دست ('hand') — ‘he drew his hand toward his sword.’
segments.s095-g21: میان plus the following noun places the action 'in the middle of/through' the brother; برادر is the object. به دو‌نیم is the resultative phrase 'into two (halves),' and کرد is the past transitive verb 'did/made' — together meaning 'he split (him) his brother in two.' The object precedes the verb in this poetic ordering.
segments.s095-g22: This is a verbless nominal description of the man just acted upon. چنان is an intensifier ('such'), followed by two adjectival compounds سنگدل (hard‑hearted) and ناهشیوار (insensible/unconscious), and finally مرد ('man'). Together they appraise him: 'such a hard‑hearted, insensible man.'
segments.s095-g23: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when.' از marks the genitive/concerned relation ('of/regarding'), so از کار اغریرث نامدار = 'of the deed (work) of Aghrirath, famed' (نامدار postmodifies the noun). This whole phrase sets the temporal/topic frame for the main clause that follows.
segments.s095-g24: خبر شد is a common classical phrasing meaning 'news happened/arrived' (i.e., 'news came'). به نزدیک here means 'to the vicinity/near,' and زال سوار is the person addressed: Zāl with the epithet/title سوار ('the rider/horseman'). The construction thus means the news reached near Zāl the rider.
segments.s095-g1: چو is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a time clause. اغریرث is a proper name; آمد is the simple past (3rd person singular) 'came'. ز آمل (z + place) is the poetic prepositional phrase 'from Amol', and به ری places the destination 'to Rey'. Word order is subject (name) + past verb + prepositional phrases.
segments.s095-g2: وزان is a poetic connective meaning 'and/of/from that', linking to previously mentioned matters; کارها is the plural 'affairs/matters'. آگهی یافت is a compound meaning 'became informed' or 'learned' (یافت is the past of 'to find/obtain'); کی here is an interrogative 'who' introducing an indirect question. The phrase order (object phrase before verb) is typical of classical Persian.
segments.s095-g3: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = past 'said'). کاین چیست is a classical question 'what is this?', with ک (relative/that) + این (this) and چیست the copula-question. کانگیختی is past/perfect of انگیختن with the prefixed k- (classical linking) meaning 'you were roused' or 'you became stirred'.
segments.s095-g4: که introduces the explanatory/subordinate clause 'that/which'; با is the instrumental 'with'. شهد حنظل is a noun phrase 'honey (شهد) of/with colocynth (حنظل)' — a proverbial mixing of sweet and bitter. برآمیختی is the past (2nd person) 'you mixed' (literally 'you mingled/combined'), agreeing with the earlier کانگیختی.
segments.s095-g5: بفرمودمت is a first-person past perfect/preterite with the second-person clitic — 'I told/commanded you' (the form can mean either 'told' or 'commanded'). کای is a vocative 'O' and برادر is the addressee 'brother'. به + کش here gives an infinitival/imperative sense 'to strike/kill/pull (the sword)', i.e. the commanded action; poetic ellipsis often omits an explicit object.
segments.s095-g6: که gives the reason 'for/because'. جای خرد نیست literally 'there is no place for wisdom' (جای = 'place/room', خرد = 'wisdom'). و هنگام هش links a second point: هنگام = 'the time/hour' and هش here denotes alarm or urgent warning; together the clause explains why striking was ordered.
segments.s095-g7: This is an inversion typical of classical Persian: به دانش نیاید = 'does not come to/does not suit' (به دانش = 'to knowledge', نیاید = negative present/potential of آمدن), followed by سر جنگجوی 'the head/of the warrior' (genitive). The sense is proverbial: learning/academic concern is not fitting for a warrior's head in this context.
segments.s095-g8: نباید is an impersonal 'one must not/it is not right to'. The following prepositional phrase 'به جنگ اندرون' means 'into internal/inner war/strife', and آبروی is the object 'honour/reputation'. The clause advises that a warrior must not risk or expose his honour in internal quarrels.

sentences.s095-s5: A warrior's head did not yield to wisdom, for wrath never blended with reason.
sentences.s095-s6: He thus answered Afrasiab that for a short while one must endure shame and (lack of) water.
sentences.s095-s7: Whenever a coat falls into bad reach, fear God and do not do evil to anyone.
sentences.s095-s8: For when crown and belt behold you, that will suffice; you will not be made tame by any person.
sentences.s095-s17: When Afrasiab heard these words — how the warlike hands had cast down the foundations.
sentences.s095-s18: He brought an army toward Khwar/Rey, arrayed for battle and pressed onward in pursuit.
sentences.s095-s19: The vanguard was in battle night and day; you said that the world was narrow for him.
sentences.s095-s20: Many fighters were slain on both sides; all the renowned men were quarrelsome.
sentences.s095-s13: He said thus: now the height of his fortune will be darkened, and his throne will be ruined.
sentences.s095-s14: He struck the iron trumpet and beat the drum; he arrayed the army like the eye of a rooster.
sentences.s095-s15: The spahbed turned his face toward Pars; he went on, furious, with a vengeful heart.
sentences.s095-s16: From sea to sea he was a man; his face was like the moon and the sun, full and round.
sentences.s095-s9: One was full of fire, another full of wisdom — how can wisdom contend with the head of a demon?
sentences.s095-s10: The spahbed flew into a rage like a drunken elephant; in reply he drew his hand to his sword.
sentences.s095-s11: He cleft his brother in two; such a hard‑hearted, insensible man.
sentences.s095-s12: When news came of the famed deed of Aghrirath, it reached the vicinity of Zāl the horseman.
sentences.s095-s1: When Aghrirath came from Amol to Rey, he learned of those affairs — who...
sentences.s095-s2: He said to him, 'What is this that you have become roused — that you mixed honey with colocynth?''
sentences.s095-s3: I had told you, O brother, to strike; for there is no room for wisdom and the hour is one of alarm.
sentences.s095-s4: Knowledge does not suit the head of a warrior; a warrior should not bring his honour into inward/household strife.
